name: Force and Pressure
subject: physics
summary: Force changes motion. Pressure is force spread over area. Nail vs sand — same idea, different feel.

crushed.title: Force = push or pull
crushed.text: Measured in newtons (N). Can start motion, stop motion, or change direction/shape.

crushed.title: Force has direction
crushed.text: It's a vector — 10 N east ≠ 10 N north. Add forces using arrows.

crushed.title: Pressure = Force ÷ Area
crushed.text: P = F/A. Same force on smaller area = higher pressure. That's why needles pierce and camels have wide feet.

crushed.title: Liquids & gases exert pressure
crushed.text: Pressure acts equally in all directions in fluids. Deeper = more pressure (more weight of fluid above).

crushed.title: Atmospheric pressure
crushed.text: Air column above you pushes ~101,325 Pa (1 atm). We don't feel it because it's balanced inside our body.

items.label: Friction
items.detail: Opposes motion between surfaces in contact

items.label: Gravitational force
items.detail: Earth pulls objects toward its center

items.label: Sharp knife
items.detail: Thin edge → small area → high pressure → easy cutting

items.label: Camel feet
items.detail: Wide pads → large area → low pressure → won't sink in sand

items.label: School bag straps
items.detail: Wide straps → lower pressure on shoulders

items.label: Deep sea diving
items.detail: Water pressure increases ~1 atm every 10 m depth

flows.title: Why a Nail Pierces Wood but Your Finger Doesn't
flows.description: Same force, different area — watch pressure change the outcome.

steps.label: You
steps.description: You push with the same force in both cases.

steps.label: Finger
steps.description: Pressure = 50/2 = 25 N/cm² — not enough to pierce wood.

steps.label: Nail tip
steps.description: Pressure = 50/0.01 = 5000 N/cm² — wood fibers break apart.

steps.label: Wood
steps.description: High pressure exceeds wood's strength locally.

steps.label: Result
steps.description: Pressure, not just force, determines whether something penetrates.

keyTerms.definition: Push or pull that can change an object's state of motion or shape
keyTerms.analogy: A nudge on a shopping cart

keyTerms.definition: Force acting per unit area (P = F/A)
keyTerms.analogy: Stiletto heel vs flat shoe on soft ground

keyTerms.definition: SI unit of pressure — 1 N per m²
keyTerms.analogy: One newton spread over a postage stamp-sized area

demos.title: Nail vs Finger — Same Force, Different Pressure
demos.description: P = F/A — a nail's tiny tip creates huge pressure and pierces wood.

steps.label: Workshop view
steps.description: Wide shot of wood block on a workbench — finger and nail ready.

steps.label: Same 50 N force
steps.description: Zoom in: identical force, but finger has large area, nail has tiny tip.

steps.label: Nail pierces wood
steps.description: High pressure (P = F/A) at the nail point splits wood fibres.

demos.title: Camel Foot on Sand
demos.description: Wide padded feet spread weight — low pressure prevents sinking.

steps.label: Camel steps
steps.description: Full body weight rests on four wide feet.

steps.label: Weight spreads
steps.description: Large contact area divides force — pressure stays low.

steps.label: Walks on sand
steps.description: Low pressure means sand doesn't collapse — camel stays on top.

chapterSummary.overview: A force is a push or pull that can change an object's motion or shape. Pressure is force per unit area (P = F/A). This chapter explains types of forces, atmospheric pressure, pressure in fluids, and everyday applications like sharp knives, camel feet, and syringes.
keyPoints: Force is a vector quantity measured in newtons (N); it has both magnitude and direction.
Pressure = Force / Area — same force on smaller area gives higher pressure.
Atmospheric pressure ≈ 101,325 Pa; we don't feel it due to internal body pressure.
Liquids and gases exert pressure equally in all directions; pressure increases with depth.
Applications: wide straps on bags, pointed nails, hydraulic brakes, suction cups.
chapterSummary.examFocus: Calculate pressure given force and area. Explain why sharp knives cut better. Describe atmospheric pressure experiments.
